title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Type 1 Trades Climb Alongside Prices
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(05th May â€“ 11th May)
  • A total of 291,453 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 32.01% week-over-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades witnessed a rise of 33.81% WoW as 1,260,250 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $58.74/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 1.15% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 7.95% in price, reaching $64.22/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price fell by 0.22% WoW; on 13th May, the spot price stood at $57.75/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $62.38/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 1.22% WoW. In contrast, D4 and D5 RIN prices rose by 1.87% WoW each. Also, D6 RIN prices increased by 0.93% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

This week marked a notable uptick in both California LCFS trading volumes and Type 1 credit prices. On the policy front, Vermont Governor Phil Scott issued Executive Order 04-25, pausing enforcement of multi-state EV sales mandates due to infrastructure and technological limitations, particularly in the heavy-duty vehicle segmentâ€”underscoring ongoing challenges in the electrification transition. At the federal level, momentum is building around clean fuels legislation. The House Ways and Means Committeeâ€™s draft proposal for the 45Z Clean Fuel Production Credit aims to tighten eligibility criteria, limit qualifying feedstocks to North America, and exclude emissions from indirect land use change in lifecycle calculationsâ€”while extending the credit through 2031 and barring access for foreign-influenced entities. Meanwhile, bipartisan support is growing for the â€œBiodiesel Tax Credit Extension Act of 2025,â€ which would extend the 40A blenderâ€™s credit, signaling continued Congressional interest in supporting domestic biofuel markets. Together, these developments signal an increasingly dynamic policy environment that will shape compliance strategies across the low-carbon fuels landscape.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_596830" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 1.15% WoW rise, increasing from $58.07 to $58.74 this week, while all non-zero credit prices increased by 7.95% WoW, from $59.49 to $64.22. Type 1 trades saw 291,453 credits exchanged, reflecting a rise of 32.01% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a rise of 33.81%, reaching 1,260,250 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_596831" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 0.22% WoW, reaching $57.80/MT. On 13th May, the average spot price was $57.75/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $62.38/MT.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_596833" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 43 trades were reported in the market, with 8 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $80.00 for one Type 2 transactions at a volume of 15,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $56.50 per credit of Type 1, reported for two transactions at Â volumes of 10,000 and 15,000 credits. The smallest volume of transfer was for 2,256 credits of the Type 1 category, undertaken for a price of $57.50. The largest volume transfers of 300,000 credits of the Type 2 category was undertaken for a price of $56.61.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_596834" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot price for LCFS Type 1 credits in April was $58.26, holding relatively steady at approximately $58.40 through the week of May 5th. Trading volumes totalled 9.12 MMT in April, with 1.65 MMT transacted through the week of May 5th.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_596835" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 05th May - 11th May, total VPT recorded a 29.56% rise WoW, with a rise in all non-zero prices.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 1.22% WoW. In contrast, D4 and D5 RIN prices rose by 1.87% WoW each. Also, D6 RIN prices increased by 0.93% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Fuel Retailers Applaud House Legislation to Extend the Biodiesel Tax Credit. NATSO, representing America's travel centers and truck stops, SIGMA: America's Leading Fuel Marketers, and the National Association of Convenience Stores (NACS), commended a bipartisan group of lawmakers for introducing the "Biodiesel Tax Credit Extension Act of 2025," which would extend the "40A" biodiesel blender's tax credit. The legislation is sponsored by Representative Mike Carey (R-Ohio) along with Representatives Andrew Garbarino (R-NY), Dusty Johnson (R-SD), Mike Kelly (R-PA), Darin LaHood (R-IL), Claudia Tenney (R-NY), and Mariannette Miller-Meeks (R-Iowa).
  • Banks, Rep. Mrvan Push for Northwest Indiana Hydrogen Hub. Senator Jim Banks (R-Ind.) and Representative Frank Mrvan (IN-01) sent a bipartisan letter to Energy Secretary Chris Wright urging the Trump administration to prioritize Northwest Indiana as a regional Hydrogen Hub. They highlighted the regionâ€™s unmatched manufacturing strength, existing energy infrastructure, and readiness to lead in blue hydrogen production using natural gas and carbon capture.Â  The lawmakers argued that investing in Indianaâ€™s hydrogen project would support President Trumpâ€™s push for American energy dominance, create jobs, lower costs, and strengthen the U.S. industrial base for decades to come.
  • Air to Jet Fuel: United Announces Investment in Power-to-Liquid Fuels Company Twelve. United announced an investment in Twelve, an innovative low carbon fuels company that uses a process similar to photosynthesis to transform CO2 and water into sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) using renewable energy. This investment by the United Airlines Ventures Sustainable Flight Fund follows Twelve's $83 million Series C funding round and project financing for its first SAF production facility, AirPlantâ„¢ One, located in Moses Lake, Washington. The facility is expected to begin production this year and plans to produce 50,000 gallons of SAF annually.
